title: Command-Line Tools
description: Practice using essential network troubleshooting command-line tools including ping, traceroute/tracert, ipconfig/ifconfig, pathping/mtr, and understanding their output for diagnosing connectivity issues.
starterCode:
# COMMAND-LINE TOOLS EXERCISE

## Part 1: Ping

1. The ping command tests _______ connectivity between two hosts by sending echo requests.
2. Ping uses the _______ (Internet Control Message Protocol) to send and receive messages.
3. ICMP echo requests are sent as Type _______ messages.
4. ICMP echo replies are received as Type _______ messages.
5. The ping command measures round-trip _______ (RTT) in milliseconds for each packet.
6. A "Request timed out" response indicates the destination is _______ or blocking ICMP.
7. The -t flag on Windows ping sends packets _______ until manually stopped with Ctrl+C.
8. The ping _______ address (127.0.0.1) tests whether the local TCP/IP stack is functioning.

## Part 2: Traceroute / Tracert

9. Traceroute displays the _______ that packets take from source to destination across the network.
10. On Windows, the traceroute command is called _______.
11. On Linux/macOS, the traceroute command is called _______.
12. Traceroute works by sending packets with incrementally increasing _______ (Time to Live) values.
13. Each router along the path _______ the TTL by one; when TTL reaches zero, it sends back an error.
14. The ICMP error message sent when TTL expires is called "Time _______."
15. An asterisk (*) in traceroute output indicates a hop that did not _______ within the timeout period.
16. Windows tracert uses _______ echo request packets, while Linux traceroute uses UDP packets by default.

## Part 3: ipconfig / ifconfig

17. The _______ command displays network configuration on Windows systems.
18. The _______ command (or 'ip addr') displays network configuration on Linux systems.
19. ipconfig /all shows detailed information including _______ address, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS servers.
20. ipconfig /_______ releases the current DHCP-assigned IP address.
21. ipconfig /_______ requests a new IP address from the DHCP server.
22. ipconfig /_______ clears the local DNS resolver cache.
23. An APIPA address starting with _______ indicates the host could not reach a DHCP server.
24. The _______ command on Linux shows the MAC (hardware) address of each network interface.

## Part 4: Pathping / MTR

25. Pathping is a Windows command that combines the functionality of _______ and traceroute.
26. Pathping computes packet _______ statistics at each hop over a period of time.
27. Pathping first discovers the route, then sends multiple _______ to each hop to calculate loss rates.
28. MTR stands for My _______ and is the Linux equivalent of pathping.
29. MTR provides a continuously updated _______ display of route and performance statistics.
30. MTR shows packet loss percentage, average latency, and _______ (variation in latency) for each hop.

## Part 5: Practical Scenarios

31. If ping to an IP address works but ping to a hostname fails, the issue is likely with _______ resolution.
32. If traceroute shows high latency starting at a specific hop, the _______ issue is likely at or near that router.
33. If ipconfig shows a 169.254.x.x address, the computer failed to get an address from the _______ server.
34. When troubleshooting, always start by pinging the _______ gateway to verify local network connectivity.
35. If ping to the default gateway succeeds but ping to external sites fails, the problem is likely with the _______ connection or routing.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: Ping

1. The ping command tests **network** connectivity between two hosts by sending echo requests.
2. Ping uses the **ICMP** (Internet Control Message Protocol) to send and receive messages.
3. ICMP echo requests are sent as Type **8** messages.
4. ICMP echo replies are received as Type **0** messages.
5. The ping command measures round-trip **time** (RTT) in milliseconds for each packet.
6. A "Request timed out" response indicates the destination is **unreachable** or blocking ICMP.
7. The -t flag on Windows ping sends packets **continuously** until manually stopped with Ctrl+C.
8. The ping **loopback** address (127.0.0.1) tests whether the local TCP/IP stack is functioning.

## Part 2: Traceroute / Tracert

9. Traceroute displays the **path** that packets take from source to destination across the network.
10. On Windows, the traceroute command is called **tracert**.
11. On Linux/macOS, the traceroute command is called **traceroute**.
12. Traceroute works by sending packets with incrementally increasing **TTL** (Time to Live) values.
13. Each router along the path **decrements** the TTL by one; when TTL reaches zero, it sends back an error.
14. The ICMP error message sent when TTL expires is called "Time **Exceeded**."
15. An asterisk (*) in traceroute output indicates a hop that did not **respond** within the timeout period.
16. Windows tracert uses **ICMP** echo request packets, while Linux traceroute uses UDP packets by default.

## Part 3: ipconfig / ifconfig

17. The **ipconfig** command displays network configuration on Windows systems.
18. The **ifconfig** command (or 'ip addr') displays network configuration on Linux systems.
19. ipconfig /all shows detailed information including **IP** address, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS servers.
20. ipconfig /**release** releases the current DHCP-assigned IP address.
21. ipconfig /**renew** requests a new IP address from the DHCP server.
22. ipconfig /**flushdns** clears the local DNS resolver cache.
23. An APIPA address starting with **169.254** indicates the host could not reach a DHCP server.
24. The **ifconfig** command on Linux shows the MAC (hardware) address of each network interface.

## Part 4: Pathping / MTR

25. Pathping is a Windows command that combines the functionality of **ping** and traceroute.
26. Pathping computes packet **loss** statistics at each hop over a period of time.
27. Pathping first discovers the route, then sends multiple **packets** to each hop to calculate loss rates.
28. MTR stands for My **Traceroute** and is the Linux equivalent of pathping.
29. MTR provides a continuously updated **real-time** display of route and performance statistics.
30. MTR shows packet loss percentage, average latency, and **jitter** (variation in latency) for each hop.

## Part 5: Practical Scenarios

31. If ping to an IP address works but ping to a hostname fails, the issue is likely with **DNS** resolution.
32. If traceroute shows high latency starting at a specific hop, the **performance** issue is likely at or near that router.
33. If ipconfig shows a 169.254.x.x address, the computer failed to get an address from the **DHCP** server.
34. When troubleshooting, always start by pinging the **default** gateway to verify local network connectivity.
35. If ping to the default gateway succeeds but ping to external sites fails, the problem is likely with the **WAN** connection or routing.
hints: Ping uses ICMP: Type 8 = echo request, Type 0 = echo reply; measures round-trip time (RTT).
Ping 127.0.0.1 (loopback) tests the local TCP/IP stack; the -t flag sends continuously on Windows.
Traceroute uses incrementing TTL values; each router decrements TTL and sends Time Exceeded when it hits 0.
Windows uses tracert (ICMP packets); Linux uses traceroute (UDP packets by default).
ipconfig (Windows) / ifconfig (Linux): /release, /renew for DHCP; /flushdns clears DNS cache.
APIPA address 169.254.x.x means the host couldn't reach a DHCP server for an IP assignment.
Pathping (Windows) combines ping + traceroute; MTR (My Traceroute) is the Linux equivalent.
MTR shows real-time packet loss, latency, and jitter statistics for each hop along the route.
If ping works by IP but not by hostname, the problem is DNS resolution.
Always ping the default gateway first — if that fails, the problem is local; if it succeeds, check WAN/routing.

testCases.description: Should correctly explain references flushdns concepts and implementation details
